cards.text: Bumiputera procurement was built for B40 Malays. The data shows it mostly reaches middlemen and T20 boards.
cards.sub: Three measurable leaks — RM12B in contract rent-seeking, RM680M in absentee board fees, race-blanket discounts that ignore income — share one pattern.
sources: T4A issue 1170 (finalScore 84.5) — Bumiputera Contract Quota System Loses RM12B to Rent-Seeking
T4A issue 1227 (finalScore 85.8) — Political Appointees in GLCs Earn RM680M Combined
T4A issue 1625 (finalScore 70.5) — Bumiputera Discount on Property Costs Non-Bumiputera Buyers RM4.8B

cards.h: RM12B a year leaks from the Bumiputera contract quota to Ali Baba middlemen — 27% of the RM45B Bumi allocation.
cards.s: Genuine Bumiputera contractors lose to commission-seekers. MACC prosecuted 42 cases in 2025 against 8,000-12,000 estimated arrangements.
cards.d: Federal government procurement totals roughly RM150 billion annually, with 30% (RM45 billion) reserved for Bumiputera-owned businesses. T4A issue 1170 documents MACC's 2025 finding that ~RM12 billion of that allocation leaks to Ali Baba arrangements — Bumi quota holders selling contracts to non-Bumi operators for 5-15% commissions. 34% of contractors surveyed admitted participating. MACC prosecuted 42 such cases in 2025 against an estimated 8,000-12,000 active arrangements nationwide. The mechanism designed to channel public spending into Bumiputera enterprise instead channels 27% of it into a commission economy.

cards.h: 342 political appointees earn RM680M from GLC boards for ~4 hours of monthly meetings each — RM1.99M average per appointee.
cards.s: 87% have no industry expertise in the GLCs they oversee. Board fees grew 34% since 2022 while worker wages in those same GLCs grew 4.8%.
cards.d: T4A issue 1227 documents 342 political appointees across 48 GLCs and statutory bodies earning a combined RM680 million in 2025. The average is RM1.99 million per appointee. 48 individuals hold 3 or more concurrent board seats, with some sitting on 5 GLCs simultaneously — averaging 4 hours of actual monthly meeting time per board. 87% lack professional qualification in the industry their GLC operates in. Board fees grew 34% since 2022 while worker wages in the same GLCs grew 4.8%. The GLC board structure — created in part to develop Bumiputera enterprise leadership — functions as a patronage layer that consumes redistribution funds rather than producing them.

cards.h: Intra-Bumiputera wealth inequality (Gini 0.39) is greater than inter-ethnic inequality (Gini 0.34) — the bigger gap is within, not between.
cards.s: Race-based policy addresses the smaller distribution. The Bumiputera property discount is not means-tested — a T20 Bumi household gets the same as a B40.
cards.d: T4A issue 1662, citing the Credit Suisse Global Wealth Report 2025, documents that intra-Bumiputera wealth inequality (Gini 0.39) exceeds inter-ethnic wealth inequality (Gini 0.34). The race-based policy framework targets the smaller distribution. T4A issue 1625 documents that the mandatory 5-15% Bumiputera property discount is not means-tested: a T20 Bumiputera household receives the same discount as a B40 household. Non-Bumi buyers cross-subsidise the discount through an estimated RM4.8 billion in higher prices in 2024 — a visible and unequal cost that produces inter-ethnic resentment while the larger intra-ethnic gap continues to widen.

cards.text: The Malay grievance isn't that Malays lose to other races. It's that programmes built for B40 Malays are captured by Malay elites.
Means-test the discounts. Hire GLC boards on merit. Track Bumiputera contracts to actual workforce, not nominal ownership. Each reform is pro-Malay — the rent-seekers are not the community.

sources: T4A issues 1170 (finalScore 84.5), 1227 (85.8), 1625 (70.5), 1662 (87.8), 1208 (84.6). Underlying primary sources: MACC 2025 investigation; governance watchdog GLC analysis 2025; REHDA / state housing data; Credit Suisse Global Wealth Report 2025; Transparency International Malaysia.
confidence: High on individual statistics (each traces to an audited T4A issue with finalScore 70.5-87.8); Medium on the synthesis framing (the 'policy capture not race' frame requires interpretation and is the load-bearing claim of the reframe card).
needs_verification: Whether the RM12B Ali Baba leak figure from issue 1170 has been updated in subsequent MACC reporting since the original 2025 investigation. If so, the hook and fact 1 should reflect the latest figure.
Whether the GLC appointee count in issue 1227 (342 across 48 GLCs) reflects 2025 or includes appointments made in 2026.
Whether the intra-Bumiputera Gini figure from issue 1662 (Credit Suisse 2025) is the most recent available; if a 2026 update exists, fact 3 should be refreshed.
source_notes: This is a synthesis issue. Its primary sources are T4A's own previously audited issues, not fresh reporting. Verification chain: this issue's card → cited T4A issue's card → that issue's brief → primary source. The cited T4A issues have finalScore range 70.5-87.8, indicating they have cleared multi-stage audit. Where the underlying issues used soft phrasing (e.g., 'estimated RM12 billion'), this synthesis preserves the hedge ('a year leaks'). No silent strengthening of underlying claims. Stage 2 (Gemini bias audit) and Stage 3 (ChatGPT fact verification) have not been independently run for this synthesis; justification is that every reused fact has already cleared an equivalent audit in its source issue.
